coredata.dc:title: High performance oxygen reduction and evolution reactions by graphitization of nickel –cobalt framework system

coredata.dc:description: This study presents the successful synthesis of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) via a simple one-pot method, followed by pyrolysis at 1000 °C to be used in the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER). A study utilizing various techniques, including field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and X-ray diffraction (XRD), investigated the properties of NiCo-MOF and its individual components before and after pyrolysis. The pristine MOFs exhibited limited electrochemical stability for both ORR and OER, while the pyrolyzed MOFs demonstrated a significant enhancement and excellent long-term stability in OER and ORR performance in alkaline media. Notably, the OER performance rivaled that of the benchmark IrO2 electrocatalyst, with similar onset potentials (1.48 V for IrO2, 1.52 V, 1.56 V, and 1.59 V for pyrolyzed NiCo-MOF, Co-MOF, and Ni-MOF, respectively). Furthermore, the pyrolyzed MOF catalysts displayed promising ORR activity in a 0.1 M KOH solution under O2 saturation, as evidenced by their onset potentials (around 0.615 V, 0.63 V, and 0.7 V vs. RHE) and favorable Tafel slopes (53, 84, and 154 mV dec⁻1, for graphitizated Ni-MOF, Co-MOF, and NiCo-MOF, respectively). These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of pyrolysis in engineering the MOF crystal structure for enhanced catalytic performance. This paves the way for further exploration of this synthesis method for the design of structurally optimized catalysts and applicable to a broad spectrum of energy technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: There have been several attempts to create a composite that retains the distinctive qualities of each individual component ORR and OER catalyst by combining outstanding non-noble metal ORR and OER electrocatalysts. This method has recently been extended to non-platinum group metals to produce bi-functional compounds for alkaline media. Additionally, it entails combining inexpensive, OER-active transition metal oxides with carbon supports that provide ORR properties for alkaline media; the activities of these mixtures are even higher than those of the noble-metal-based electrocatalysts [ 21\u201324].Furthermore, MOFs have porous properties that enable them to serve as host materials for anchoring nanoclusters or metal single atoms, thereby enhancing electrocatalytic activity. In addition, MOFs can also be modified by coupling them with other functional nanomaterials. It is also important to note that MOF-based skeletons provide the possibility of rearrangement of elements at the molecular and atomic levels during post-processing, making MOFs a promising precursor for the development of more efficient electrocatalysts [32, 36\u201342]. For example, nitrogen-doped porous carbon formed from ZIF-7, single Co atoms supported on N-doped carbon via bimetallic Zn/Co MOF, and Fe3C@N-CNT derived from MIL-88B@ZIF-8 dual-MOF composites were found to perform well in terms of ORR [43,44].

head.ce:title: High performance oxygen reduction and evolution reactions by graphitization of nickel –cobalt framework system

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents the successful synthesis of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) via a simple one-pot method, followed by pyrolysis at 1000 °C to be used in the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER). A study utilizing various techniques, including field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and X-ray diffraction (XRD), investigated the properties of NiCo-MOF and its individual components before and after pyrolysis.

The pristine MOFs exhibited limited electrochemical stability for both ORR and OER, while the pyrolyzed MOFs demonstrated a significant enhancement and excellent long-term stability in OER and ORR performance in alkaline media. Notably, the OER performance rivaled that of the benchmark IrO2 electrocatalyst, with similar onset potentials (1.48 V for IrO2, 1.52 V, 1.56 V, and 1.59 V for pyrolyzed NiCo-MOF, Co-MOF, and Ni-MOF, respectively). Furthermore, the pyrolyzed MOF catalysts displayed promising ORR activity in a 0.1 M KOH solution under O2 saturation, as evidenced by their onset potentials (around 0.615 V, 0.63 V, and 0.7 V vs. RHE) and favorable Tafel slopes (53, 84, and 154 mV dec⁻1, for graphitizated Ni-MOF, Co-MOF, and NiCo-MOF, respectively). These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of pyrolysis in engineering the MOF crystal structure for enhanced catalytic performance. This paves the way for further exploration of this synthesis method for the design of structurally optimized catalysts and applicable to a broad spectrum of energy technologies.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the combustion of fossil fuels and the rapid growth of energy demand, reproducible energy conversion and storage devices have a wide range of applications as clean, pollution-free, and sustainable [1–3]. The most promising alternatives to fossil fuels have been identified as the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) [4], oxygen evolution reaction (OER) [5], and the hydrogen production [6]. These processes are at the heart of future renewable and environmentally friendly energy technologies, including fuel cells, metal-air batteries, and water splitting [2,7,8]. Thus, the development of highly efficient bifunctional electrocatalysts for ORR and OER is the key to these applications [9–11].

Currently, platinum-based materials display excellent ORR performance, whereas noble metal oxides (RuO2 and IrO2) are benchmark electrocatalysts for OER. Due to their high price, low availability, instability, and inferior poison tolerance of Pt itself, precious metals are difficult to use in practical applications. As a result, it is essential to develop a non-precious metal-based bifunctional electrocatalyst with abundant natural resources, low cost, and high stability [12–20].

There have been several attempts to create a composite that retains the distinctive qualities of each individual component ORR and OER catalyst by combining outstanding non-noble metal ORR and OER electrocatalysts. This method has recently been extended to non-platinum group metals to produce bi-functional compounds for alkaline media. Additionally, it entails combining inexpensive, OER-active transition metal oxides with carbon supports that provide ORR properties for alkaline media; the activities of these mixtures are even higher than those of the noble-metal-based electrocatalysts [21–24].

Todays, heteroatom-doped carbon materials have much attention because of their strong electrochemical properties. Heteroatoms such as N, P, S, B, and halogens can alter the electrical structure and surface polarity of carbon, leading to active sites [25–27]. On the other side, transition metals such as Co, Fe, Ni, Mo, Mn, and Zn, etc. have the advantage of being inexpensive, non-toxic, and abundant in the earth, but they often exhibit high over potentials and low stability during water splitting and 0RR. In light of these factors, the focus of research is to increase the rate of electrocatalytic reaction of non-precious metals so as to obtain lower overpotentials and greater stability in the carbon structure [24,28–31]. Recently, CeE (E = S, Se) chalcogenides [31] and Nd-dopped CeO2 (CeNdO2) nanostructures co-doped with rare earth metals [30] are fabricated as efficiant electrocatalysts to be used in OER.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), which are composed of metal nodes and organic ligands with strong coordination bonds, have been concerned due to their unique structural characteristics and intrinsically large surface area [32–34]. As a result of their diverse architectures and rich active sites, MOFs are getting a great deal of attention from scientists in the field of electrocatalytic energy conversion, especially in the field of electrochemical ORR, OER, and overal water splitting because of their large surface area and hierarchical pore structures [30,35]. For example, Rashid at, al. synthesized MnCe3.67C6 catalyst by using pyrolysis process. This catalyst is particularly effective for OER activity with a requiring a much lower potential at current density of 10 mAcm−2 in alkaline media [35].

Furthermore, MOFs have porous properties that enable them to serve as host materials for anchoring nanoclusters or metal single atoms, thereby enhancing electrocatalytic activity. In addition, MOFs can also be modified by coupling them with other functional nanomaterials. It is also important to note that MOF-based skeletons provide the possibility of rearrangement of elements at the molecular and atomic levels during post-processing, making MOFs a promising precursor for the development of more efficient electrocatalysts [32,36–42]. For example, nitrogen-doped porous carbon formed from ZIF-7, single Co atoms supported on N-doped carbon via bimetallic Zn/Co MOF, and Fe3C@N-CNT derived from MIL-88B@ZIF-8 dual-MOF composites were found to perform well in terms of ORR [43,44].

Co-MOFs, Fe-MOFs, and Cu-MOFs, have been reported to exhibit outstanding OER catalytic activity [45]. In spite of the fact that these MOFs exhibit good electrochemical performance in light of their interlayer structure, a limited number of reports have been published concerning their use [46].Meanwhile, mixed-metal MOFs with more than one metal anywhere in their structure show better performance in various applications than single-metal MOFs as a result of their synergistic effect. As a mixed-metal photocatalyst, bimetallic MOFs with organic ligands coordinating with two different metals exhibit a single-phase structure similar to single-metal MOFs, but possess better flexibility than single-metal MOFs. Researchers have noticed that bimetallic MOFs can be tuned to achieve the desired properties by adjusting the ratio between two different metals, which has attracted their attention [47]. It has been recently shown that the synergistic mechanism of the Co–Ni model plays an important role in regulating electrocatalytic activity via the coupling between Ni and Co metals [45].In that regard, Wang et al. [48], prepared Ni/Co MOF via a hydrothermal process using Ni2+ and Co2+ as metal nodes and 4,4 biphenyl dicarboxylic as the organic ligand. With different current densities, Ni/Co MOF nanorods display prominent pseudocapacitive behavior and high specific capacitance. The hierarchical CoNi2-MOF synthesized by Bu et al. [49], displayed a high level of activity and durability towards OER, with an overpotential of 240 mV at 10 mA cm-2 and a Tafel slope of 58 mV dec−1.

However, their insulating nature (lack of electrical conductivity) and low chemical stability prevent the majority of existing MOFs from successfully being used in electrocatalysis, which is central to energy storage and generation applications [14,15]. In order to address this challenge, MOFs or MOF composites can be transformed into inorganic composites through carbonization or indirect post-treatments. Despite MOFs' structural and compositional advantages, their derivatives are generally characterized by high surface areas, high electrical conductivity, and excellent chemical stability [17]. According to these guidelines, a pyrolyzed CoNi-MOF electrocatalyst for highly efficient water splitting and ORR was developed. This catalyst has not been previously reported as bifunctional electrocatalyst for OERand ORR. The pyrolyzed CoNi-MOF electrocatalyst demonstrates promising ORR and OER activity, making it a good candidate for various energy applications. Furthermore, its performance can be further optimized by incorporating different metals and dopants, opening exciting avenues for future research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Co-MOF, Ni-MOF, and CoNi-MOF
ce:section.ce:para: A facile hydrothermal method was employed for the synthesis of a NiCo-based metal-organic framework (MOF). In a typical procedure, 51.2 mL of DMF, 6.4 mL of ethanol, and 6.4 mL of water were mixed in a 100 mL beaker at room temperature. Subsequently, equimolar quantities of Co(NO3)2.6H2O and Ni(NO3)2.6H2O (0.58 g) were added and kept under vigorous stirring for minutes. Then, 0.25 mmol of terephthalic acid (TPA) and 0.15 mmol tri ethanol amin (TEA) were dissolved in the mixture under continued stirring for a designated time (h). The as-prepared solution was transferred into a 50 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 140 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting light brown precipitate was washed three times with DMF and ethanol, followed by filtration and drying in an oven at 80 °C for 15 h. The obtained powder was designated as a CoNi-MOF catalyst. For comparison, Co-MOF and Ni-MOF were synthesized using the same procedure, with the sole modification of employing only Co(NO3)2•6H2O or Ni(NO3)2•6H2O salts, respectively.

The obtained MOFs were converted into nanocarbons via temperature-programmed pyrolysis (TPP) under a nitrogen atmosphere. Typically, 0.5 g of MOF was heated in a tube furnace under N2 flow at a rate of 10 °C min-1 to a final temperature ranging from 600 °C to 900 °C. The sample was then held at the peak temperature for 10 min before being allowed to cool down to room temperature. The resulting materials were designated as MOF-T1 for Ni-MOF, MOF-T2 for Co-MOF, and MOF-T3 for CoNi-MOF, where T is the carbonization temperature.

Electrochemical measurements were performed using a potentiostat-galvanostat in a three-electrode configuration. A platinum counter electrode, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode (KCl 1 M), and a glassy carbon working electrode were employed. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was conducted in a N2-saturated alkaline electrolyte within a potential window of 0.3–1.9 V vs. RHE. The working electrode consisted of glassy carbon modified with the synthesized catalysts, with a geometric area of 0.042 cm2. A circulating water bath maintained the cell temperature by controlling the flow of heated or cooled water through the cell jacket. All potentials were referenced to the measured reversible hydrogen electrode potential (RHE). ORR activity was evaluated in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH and OER in a 1 M KOH solution. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements were recorded at a scan rate of 5 mV s⁻1.

The crystal structures of the as-prepared samples were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 1 (a) , the prepared Ni-MOF sample matches the crystal structure of the monoclinic Ni2(OH)2BDC MOF (no. 985792, Cambridge Crystallographic Data Centre) and Ni-MOF (CCDC 638,866) [50,51]. The four main diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 8.82°, 14.16°, 15.7°, and17.9°, attributed to (200), (001), (201) and (−201) crystallographic planes, respectively [52]. This point out the highly crystalline nature of the catalyst. The XRD patterns of the Co-MOF align well with previously reported data [46,53,54]. The XRD characteristic peaks of Co-MOF located at 2θ = 8.92°,15.82°, 17.74° and 30.86° were highly consist with the CCDC 153067 (Co-MOF) [51,55] Inaddition, The presence of certain peaks at 30°–50° suggests that the Ni-MOF and Co-MOF nanocatalysts have a well-ordered crystalline with well crystalline [56]. The positions and intensities of these peaks correspond to the spacing between atomic planes within MOF crystals. In fact, examining the most intense peaks is sufficient to determine the MOF structure. Any additional peaks or peaks that have shifted could indicate the presence of impurities or changes in the structure [57].

XRD patterns of the NiCo-MOF exhibit a similar pattern to Ni-MOF, but with some additional peaks due to the presence of cobalt, confirming the purity and equal occupancy of metal centers by Co2⁺ and Ni2⁺ [58].

The XRD patterns of carbonized products of MOF-T1, MOF-T2, and MOF-T3 are shown in Fig. 1 (b). These patterns reveal characteristic peaks corresponding to crystalline metal oxide nanoparticles embedded within the carbon matrix. For carbonized Ni-MOF or MOF-T1, the XRD pattern typically shows characteristic diffraction lines at 37.30°, 43.33°, 62.91°, 75.42°, and 79.39°, corresponding to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0), (3 1 1) and (2 2 2) crystal planes of cubic NiO species, respectively [59]. This indicates the well-crystalline nature of Ni/C with a significant amount of NiO species.

In the case of carbonized cobalt MOF (MOF-T2), diffraction peaks can be observed at 31.27°, 36.86°, 44.81°, 59.35°, and 65.22° indexed to the (220), (311), (400), (511), and (440) planes of the cubic Co3O4 phase, matching the standard JCPDS data (ICDD 00-042-1467) [60,61]. Additionally, weak peaks corresponding to CoO (JCPDS no. 01–1227) are likely due to the oxidation of small Co nanoparticles during air exposure. For graphitization of NiCo-MOF or MOF-T3, the XRD pattern typically shows a combination of the peaks observed for MOF-T1 and MOF-T2, indicating the presence of both nickel oxide and cobalt oxide nanoparticles within the carbon matrix. Following decomposition in an inert atmosphere, the XRD pattern of the NiCo-MOF sample suggests the formation of NiO species (Fig. 1 (b)). Moreover, some weak peaks assigned to Co3O4 are also observed in the samples. The XRD patterns of the MOFs are in good agreement with the structure of the reported MOFs in the literature [62,63].
Also the results reveal that NiCo-MOF has been converted into graphitic carbon materials integrating various Co3O4 and NiO species, which is favorable for ORR and OER catalysis [64].

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were employed to analyze the surface area and pore structure of the as-synthesized materials using the BET method. As depicted in Fig. S1, the isotherm exhibits a type IV profile with H3 hysteresis loop, signifying the presence of mesopores within the material [65,66].

Fig. 2 shows the infrared spectrum for the Co-MOF, Ni-MOF, and NiCo-MOF before breakdown and their decomposition, including MOF-T1, MOF-T2, and MOF-T3 in the range of 4000−400 cm−1. The high degree of similarity between the spectra of all samples before decomposition suggests a comparable structural framework (Fig. 2). The stretching vibrations of para-aromatic CH and OH− groups give bands around 1498 and 3601 cm−1, respectively. The intense bands at 1579 cm−1 and 1353 cm−1 can be assigned to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the carboxylate (COO⁻) group, indicating its coordination with the metal centers. These peaks can represent Co–COO–Co, Ni–COO–Ni, or bimetallic Co–COO–Ni functionalities as reported in the literature [55,56] and the peak at 1145 cm−1 corresponds to the C–O vibration modes of interlayer MOF molecules [57]. The appearance of peaks related to the vibrations of the M−O and M−O−M (M = Ni and Co) at 471, 603 and 693 cm−1 suggests the formation of a metal-oxo bond between the (Ni or Co) atoms and the carboxylic group of the H2BDC ligand [55]. These peaks are attributed to the out-of-plane aromatic bending vibrations and prove that TPA led to good coordination with nickel and cobalt ions. Regarding the low-wavenumber region, the absorptions are around 450 cm−1, which can be ascribed to metal–oxygen–hydrogen bending vibration (M − OH, where M = Co, Ni, or both in the case of NiCo-MOF). This result indicates that NiCo-MOF material has been formed, which consists of two metal centers (Ni2+, Co2+) coordinated with an organic linker (BDC2−) [58,59]. The intense peaks at 743 cm−1 and 808 cm−1 are assigned to the aromatic C–H bend vibrations, and band at 1015 cm−1 is assigned to the C–H group in plane bending vibration [60]. The FTIR spectra of MOF-T1, T2, and T3 exhibit significant changes compared to their corresponding Ni-MOF, Co-MOF, and NiCo-MOF materials. Notably, peaks characteristic of OH- and CO functional groups are entirely absent in the decomposed materials. These spectral alterations signify the breakdown of the original MOF structure. However, residual metal-oxygen vibrations persist in the 400-750 cm−1 region, suggesting the presence of remnant metal centers. In MOF-T2 sample, the stretching vibrational band at 500 cm−1 corresponds to the cobalt-oxygen (Co–O) bond [67]. In MOF-T1 the absorption band at 552 cm−1 and 651 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibrations of nickel-oxygen (Ni–O) bonds [68]. MOF-T3 shows a similar pattern, with an absorption peak at 670 cm−1 corresponding to Ni–O bonds and a weaker peak at 490 cm−1 attributed to Co–O bonds.

SEM, EDX, and TEM analysis were also used to define the morphology of the powders and their compositional homogeneity, before and after decomposition. Fig. 3 a,b, and c show the SEM images of Co-MOF, Ni-MOF, and NiCo-MOF samples. SEM images show that the crystal sizes of MOF samples were about 1∼5 μm. All samples have a usually plate-like crystalline structure that is fine and uniform, indicating an ordered arrangement of atoms within the MOFs, varies in size and diameter, and shows obvious accumulation of nanosheets. However, the Co-MOF (a) has pronounced and sharp crystal formations, indicating a high degree of crystallinity that may influence its porosity and surface area, whereas the Ni- MOF (b) has smoother surfaces and less defined edges, which could be indicative of a different growth mechanism or interaction between the Co ions and the organic linkers. The NiCo- MOF (c) appears to have a hybrid shape, incorporating elements of both Ni and Co MOFs. This shows a synergistic effect of the two metals on crystal formation, which could lead to novel catalytic or adsorption capabilities.

EDS diagrams for three samples are shown in Fig. 3i, ii, and iii. The EDS of each element in a sample demonstrates that all metals are evenly distributed inside the area of the crystalline particles. This clearly shows the existence of Co, N, O, and C in the Co-MOF sample, however, in Ni-MOF, the Co element has been substituted with Ni, and in NiCo-MOF, in addition to N, C, and O, Co and Ni are also present.

Fig. 4 shows the SEM topography of Co-MOF (a), Ni-MOF (b), and NiCo-MOF (c) after decomposition and destruction. The decomposition process has caused the loss of fine details and uniformity in the original MOFs. Despite decomposition, remnants of the porous nature of MOFs are still visible in all three images.

Fig. 4i, ii, and iii show EDS diagrams of three samples after degradation. EDS is almost comparable to before destruction, and no significant alteration is detected. The dramatic increase in the carbon fraction and corresponding decrease in oxygen content, along with the constant total metal content (though their relative percentage increases after degradation), convincingly demonstrate successful carbonization.

Fig. 5 (a and b) shows TEM images of the NiCo-MOF before decomposition (a) and MOF-T3 after decomposition (b) with 2 scales. TEM images highlight the effect of decomposition on MOF structures.The pristine NiCo-MOF (a) exhibits well-defined crystalline features, with the TEM image revealing cubic-shaped crystals. In contrast, the MOF-T3 (b) shows a significant loss of crystallinity and increased amorphousness after pyrolysis, indicating structural collapse. Interestingly, despite the collapse, some multifaceted structures in the nano-range of 5 and 40 nm are still observable in the TEM image of MOF-T3.

To assess the electrocatalytic properties of our catalysts for ORR and OER, we used cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) on a rotating disk electrode (RDE) in alkaline media at room temperature. We also measured commercial 20 wt% Pt/C, and and IrO2 catalysts for comparison. Fig. 6 a and b shows the linear voltammogram (LSV) of the different electrodes in the presence of N2 in 1 M KOH solution at a scan rate of 5 mV/s with a catalyst loading of approximately 0.2 mg cm−2. Fig. 6a shows MOF LSV curves after 20 cycles of continuous CV scanning. The CV was performed from 0.3 to 1.8 V vs RHE. After 20 cycles, it is clear that Co-MOF, Ni-MOF, and NiCo-MOF show very poor electrocatalytic activity. From these experiments, Tafel slope values were found to be 143, 135, and 120 mV/dec for Ni-MOF, Co-MOF, and NiCo-MOF respectively. The related linear Tafel graphs are displayed in inset Fig. 6a. Comparing the overpotential (ƞ) for reaching a current density of 10 mA cm−2 as a useful metric reference for solar fuel production might be helpful [61]. According to the standard potential of 1.23 V for the electrolysis of water to oxygen, the overpotentials of Ni-MOF, Co-MOF, and NiCo-MOF were found to have values of 0.49, 0.45, and 0.40 V RHE, respectively. The polarization curve for decomposition MOF materials and benchmark IrO2 is shown in Fig. 6b. The onset potentials are 1.48, 1.52 V, 1.56V and 1.59V for IrO2, MOF-T3, MOF-T2, and MOF-T1, respectively, which are very lower than those before pyrolysis. For example, the current density at 1.7 V for decompsited NiCo-MOF or MOF-T3 is 44 mA cm−2, 3.6 times higher than that for NiCo-MOF (12 mA cm−2), demonstrating that graphitization plays a critical role in obtaining high performance electrocatalysts for OER. While the OER activity may not be the highest compared to Refs. [69,70], the prepared material demonstrates superior overall performance due to its bifunctional nature, catalyzing both OER and ORR effectively.

Fig. 7 depicts the CV of MOF-T3 in a 0.1 M KOH solution at 50 mV/s from 0.3 to 1, 1.2, 1.4, 1.6, and 1.8 V. As seen in the potential window between 0 and 1 V, there is no peak in the cathodic or anodic regions. By increasing the potential to 1.2 and 1.4, the cathodic and anodic peaks for Co(II)/Co(III) between 0.9 and 1.2 V vs. RHE may be observed. As a result of increasing the voltage to 1.6 and 1.8 V in addition to completing the Co(II)/Co(III) peaks, the OER water wall at abow 1.5 V and ORR peaks at less than 0.6 V vs. RHE were seen, confirming the NiCo/CN bifunctional electrocatalyst capabilities in OER and ORR (inset Fig. 7).

This work describes the successful synthesis of Ni-MOF, Co-MOF, and NiCo-MOF via a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. Subsequent pyrolysis at 900 °C yielded multifaceted electrocatalysts with a very high surface area-to- volume ratio, exhibiting potential for both oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) applications.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements revealed enhanced ORR and OER electrocatalytic activities for the pyrolyzed MOFs compared to their pristine counterparts in alkaline media. Notably, the pyrolyzed NiCo-MOF demonstrated comparable ORR electrocatalytic performance to the benchmark commercial 10% Pt/C catalyst. This conclusion is supported by the oxygen reduction peak potential observed in the CV and the Tafel slope of nearly 53 mV dec⁻1 obtained from the LSV measurements.

Furthermore, the experimental results demonstrated superior OER performance and comparable electrochemical stability of the pyrolyzed NiCo-MOF compared to the pristine form. Notably, the stability of the pyrolyzed NiCo-MOF is similar to that of the IrO2 catalyst for OER. These findings highlight the promise of this novel Co2⁺-based mixed transition metal oxide composite as a bifunctional electrocatalyst for energy conversion applications. Finally, the acceptable oxygen reduction and evolution activity of the pyrolyzed NiCo-MOF electrocatalyst opens doors to its application in a broad spectrum of energy devices.

Karim Kakaei: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization. Aida Rahmati: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Investigation, Data curation. Mohammad Ghadiri: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Fatemeh Asgharnezhad: Software, Formal analysis, Data curation.
